items.title: ICKL-7
items.subtitle: 7th International Conference on Kurdish Linguistics
items.personal_note: Here, Raman and I presented our ongoing research efforts that grew and evolved from our stunt at the ICKL-6 2 years prior. We had a fantastic time and met even more great people than the first time! We can't wait to to attent the 8th ICKL in two years! :D

images.caption: Conference aimed at the low-resource language Kurdish and any neighboring or contacting languages, with a linguistic focus.

presentations.title: Dialectal Filtering: Synthesize Text Corpora for Kurdish Varieties by Enriching Datasets with Multi-Labels
presentations.description: Kurdish, a low-resource language with extensive dialectal variation, faces significant challenges in NLP due to noisy, mixed-dialect text corpora. This research introduces a dialect-aware text filtering framework to preprocess and enrich Kurdish datasets, synthesizing variety-specific sub-corpora for underrepresented dialects (see Figure 1). By leveraging lexicographic features, this approach generates over 100 sub-corpora from Northern Kurdish data, offering resources for dialect-specific NLP tasks but has currently limitations in precision.

image.caption: (Placeholder picture) Christian Schuler, Raman Ahmad, Ānrán Wáng

presentations.title: The Ezafe as an Aspectual Marker in Copular Constructions of the Kobani Variety: Stage-Level vs. Individual-Level Predicates
presentations.description: This work investigates the use of tense ezafe (î for masculine singular, e for feminine singular/plural) in copula constructions of Kobani, a Southwestern Kurmanji variety spoken in Rojava. Aligning with other Western Kurmanji dialects like Maraş, Kobani’s tense ezafe encodes stage-level predicates and subject agreement. Unlike standard Kurmanji, which lacks tense ezafe or some dialects like Afrini, where ezafe reportedly marks verbal predicates (e.g., ez-î derim ‘I am going’), Kobani restricts this to copular phrases, contributing to our understanding of morphosyntactic diversity within Kurmanji dialects.

image.alt: ICKL-7 Ezafe in Copular Constructions of Kobani
image.caption: Due to visa-difficulties, Raman and I had the entire poster-session just for us, which kept us excitingly busy with discussions!

images.caption: PhD school that crosses many disciplines with speakers from diverse research areas to deliver their stories on use of probability in computer science.
Apparently, the budget could not cover printing event posters or signs - but credit where credit is due, once they started serving pastries during the breaks, everyone's spirit rose to the skies ;)
The Spandauer (sweet pastry) alone are worth the travel to Denmark! :D
A yearly event for students to display and discuss the outcomes of their projects and theses.

items.personal_note: Here, Raman and I presented our projects as part of the poster session. We had an amazing time and got to meet so many awesome people that we can't wait to see again in two years at the 7th ICKL! :D

images.caption: Conference aimed at the low-resource language Kurdish and any neighboring or contacting languages, with a linguistic focus.

presentations.title: Analysis of Phonology and Morphology in the Kobani Dialect
presentations.description: Kobanî is a Kurdish dialect spoken in and around Kobani (Ain al-Arab), which is the capital city of the district 'Ain al-'Arab in the governement Aleppo in Syria. The Kobanî dialect can be considered to be part of the Southern Kurmanji dialect-group and has approximately 200,000 native speakers. This is a computer-aided analysis of the Kurdish dialect Kobani, based on speech data collected from dozens of native speakers from various villages of that region.

presentations.title: Towards a Complete Mapping of Kurdish Dialectology
presentations.description: There exist a plathora of Kurdish dialects that are often unintelligible to the native speakers of each other. However, Kurdish is considered to be a dialect continuum with at times sudden language shifts and sometimes smooth transitions from one dialect to the neighboring one. At first it might seem confusing and counter intuitive that it has been found, that Central Kurdish is considered to be very understandable by speakers of Northern Kurdish and Southern Kurdish to be understandable by speakers of Central Kurdish- but speakers of Northern Kurdish hardly being able to understand Southern Kurdish. An explanation for this can be found in the prior mentioned dialect continuum- where 'Central Kurdish' is not necessarily 'Central Kurdish', since there exist many differen subdialects of Central Kurdish. Which are there, where can they be found, is what we try to figure out here.
